name: Cannonball River
short_description: Hunt for petrified wood and colorful agates along the Cannonball River in northwestern North Dakota. This flat, accessible public land site offers excellent collecting for beginners and families.
description:
The Cannonball River drainage in Sioux County, North Dakota, is a productive rockhounding destination known for petrified wood specimens and silica-based gemstones. The river's extensive exposures of Paleocene-era sedimentary formations have weathered to reveal beautiful agate, jasper, and chalcedony pieces along with scattered petrified wood fragments. The flat terrain and readily accessible riverbank make this site ideal for rockhounds of all experience levels.

The geology of this region reflects ancient floodplain and lacustrine deposits from the Fort Union Formation, laid down roughly 60-65 million years ago during the early Paleocene epoch. The area has experienced significant erosion and weathering over millennia, exposing colorful agate nodules and jasper specimens that weather out of the hillsides and collect in river gravels. Petrified wood is particularly common here, representing fossilized trees from ancient forests that were buried and replaced by silica.

Visitors typically walk short distances from parking areas along the river valley, searching exposed banks, gravel bars, and weathered slopes for loose specimens. The flat elevation and lack of significant elevation gain make this an excellent beginner site. Specimens range from small thumb-sized agates to larger chunks of petrified wood suitable for display or cutting.

The Cannonball River area is part of the vast prairie landscape of northwestern North Dakota, characterized by grassland, badlands exposures, and riparian vegetation along water courses. Collecting here offers both geological education and tangible rewards without requiring extensive hiking or technical skill.
history: The Cannonball River drainage sits within the Fort Union Formation, a prolific Paleocene-age sedimentary sequence that blankets much of the northern Great Plains. Ancient rivers and floodplains deposited silica-rich sediments 60-65 million years ago, and groundwater rich in dissolved silica later replaced wood and formed agate nodules within these deposits. Weathering and erosion over millions of years have exposed these gemstones at the surface, making them accessible to modern rockhounders.
tips: Search the exposed riverbanks and gravel bars where recent erosion has washed loose specimens downslope - this is where you will find the most accessible material. Look for translucent or banded agate pieces and darker petrified wood with visible growth rings or grain structure. Bring a hand lens to examine banding patterns and confirm agate versus chalcedony. A small hammer or chisel helps pry specimens from partially exposed positions, but much material lies loose on the surface ready to collect.
terrain_notes: The Cannonball River valley is characterized by flat to gently rolling terrain at 1,693 feet elevation. The riverbank consists of gravel bars, exposed clay and sandstone banks, and sparse grassland vegetation typical of the northern Great Plains. Walking distances from parking areas are typically short (under one mile). The footing is generally firm on gravel and clay surfaces, though loose material and some wet areas may be encountered after recent precipitation. No significant elevation gain is involved in typical collecting walks.
best_season: April - October. Spring through fall offers the most comfortable weather and accessible conditions. Summer can bring extreme heat and thunderstorm activity. Winter snows and cold reduce accessibility and comfort. Late spring and early fall provide the most pleasant conditions for extended collecting sessions.
written_directions: From Marmarth (the nearest town, approximately 18 miles south), take US Highway 2 north toward Williston. Continue past the Sioux/Slope County line. The Cannonball River crosses US 2 near its intersection with county roads in Sioux County. Look for informal pullouts and access points along US 2 near river crossings, or turn east on local county roads (check current maps for latest road designations) that provide closer access to the river valley. Approach via passenger vehicle if roads are dry; some approaches may require high-clearance vehicles after wet weather.
road_conditions: US Highway 2 is paved. County road approaches vary; some may be gravel or dirt, passable by passenger vehicle in dry conditions. Check local conditions before visiting in wet weather.
parking_notes: Informal pullouts and parking areas exist along US Highway 2 near river crossings and along county roads providing access to the Cannonball River drainage. Capacity and amenities are minimal; no developed parking areas are available.
collection_rules: This site is on public land. Personal use collection only. Maximum 25 lbs per day per federal land use regulations. No commercial collection. No motorized excavation. No removal of fossils or paleontological specimens - petrified wood fragments and agate are acceptable; actual fossil bone or teeth should be left in place. Pack out all trash. Respect any posted closures or seasonal restrictions.
rules:
Personal use only
Maximum 25 lbs per day
No commercial collection
No motorized excavation
No removing paleontological fossils
Pack out all trash
Respect posted seasonal closures
nearest_services: Marmarth, North Dakota (approximately 18 miles south on US 2) offers gas, food, and basic supplies. Williston is approximately 50 miles north and provides full services.
accessibility_notes: Flat terrain accessible to most hikers and families. Short walking distances from parking areas (typically under one mile). No wheelchair access due to uneven gravel and riverbank surfaces. Sturdy footwear recommended. Weather and moisture can affect footing - avoid after heavy rain when clay banks become slippery.

faq.question: What types of gemstones can you find at Cannonball River in North Dakota?
faq.answer: The primary finds at Cannonball River are petrified wood, agate, jasper, and chalcedony. Most specimens are small to medium-sized; agates typically range from thumb-sized to several inches across. Petrified wood is abundant and often displays visible growth rings or grain patterns. Agate pieces frequently show banding in shades of brown, tan, red, and white.

faq.question: Is rockhounding at Cannonball River free or do you need to pay a fee?
faq.answer: Rockhounding at Cannonball River is completely free. It is public land with no access fees or entrance charges. You only need to follow basic personal use collection rules: maximum 25 lbs per day, no commercial collecting, and no motorized excavation.

faq.question: Do I need a permit to collect rocks at Cannonball River, North Dakota?
faq.answer: No permit is required for personal use rockhounding at Cannonball River. However, you must follow federal public land regulations: collect only for personal use, limit yourself to 25 lbs per day, and do not remove paleontological fossils such as fossil bone or teeth.

faq.question: What is the best time of year to rockhound at Cannonball River?
faq.answer: The best season at Cannonball River is April through October. Spring and fall offer the most comfortable weather and accessible conditions. Summer can bring extreme heat and thunderstorms, while winter snow and cold reduce accessibility. Late May through September provides ideal collecting conditions.

faq.question: How far is Cannonball River from nearby towns and what should I bring?
faq.answer: Cannonball River is approximately 18 miles north of Marmarth, North Dakota via US Highway 2. Bring water, sun protection, sturdy shoes, and a small hammer or chisel for loosening specimens. A hand lens helps identify agate banding. Since services are limited, fill up on gas and food in Marmarth before heading north.

faq.question: Is Cannonball River a good beginner rockhounding site for families and kids?
faq.answer: Yes, Cannonball River is excellent for beginners and families. The flat terrain requires minimal hiking, most specimens are found loose on the surface or in gravel bars, and children ages 6 and up can easily participate. The short walking distances and lack of technical skill requirements make it perfect for introducing family members to rockhounding.

meta_description: Find petrified wood, agate, and jasper at Cannonball River. Free public land rockhounding in Sioux County with easy access and excellent collecting for beginners.
